coredata.dc:title: Graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets anchored with BiOBr and carbon dots: Exceptional visible-light-driven photocatalytic performances for oxidation and reduction reactions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Asadzadeh-Khaneghah, Soheila
Habibi-Yangjeh, Aziz
Nakata, Kazuya
coredata.dc:description: The aim of this work is the fabrication of very efficient visible-light-driven photocatalysts through anchoring carbon dots and BiOBr over nanosheets of graphitic carbon nitride. Hence, a series of g-C3N4 nanosheets/Carbon dots/BiOBr (denoted as CNNS/CDs/BiOBr) nanocomposites with different amounts of BiOBr were synthesized by refluxing route. The resultant photocatalysts were characterized by XRD, EDX, SEM, TEM, HRTEM, AFM, XPS, FT-IR, UV–vis DRS, TGA, BET, and PL instruments. Among the photocatalysts, the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance for degradations of RhB, MB, and MO under visible light, which is about 129, 29.8, and 20.5 times as superior as the CN powder, respectively. Also, photoreduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) over the ternary nanocomposite with 20% of BiOBr showed high photoactivity, which was 21.7-folds higher than that of the pristine CN. Reactive species trapping experiments revealed that superoxide anion radicals and hydroxyl radicals played significant role in degradation reaction of RhB. The utilized photocatalyst was recycled with negligible loss in the activity, which is important in photocatalytic processes. Finally, by studying the electrochemical behavior, the band alignments of the semiconductors were obtained and the preliminary mechanism was suggested as a direct Z‐scheme system for the enhanced interfacial charge separation and transfer, which leads to the exceptional photocatalytic performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4-NS/CDs/BiOBr
Photocatalysis
Reduction of Cr(VI)
Visible-light photocatalyst
Z-scheme mechanism

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors genuinely acknowledge University of Mohaghegh Ardabili for financial support of this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns for the CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, CNNS/BiOBr (20%), and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites with different weight percentages of BiOBr.
(a) EDX spectra for the CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) samples. (b–g) EDX mapping of the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite.
(a) SEM image of CNNS, (b) SEM, (c) TEM, and (d) HRTEM images of the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite.
(a) AFM image of the CNNS sample, and (b) the corresponding height image of two random nanosheets.
XPS spectra for the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite: (a) survey scan, and high-resolution spectra for: (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) Bi 4f, and (f) Br 3d.
FT-IR spectra for the CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, CNNS/BiOBr (20%), and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) samples.

(a) UV–vis DR spectra for the CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, CNNS/BiOBr (20%), and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites. (b) Plots of (αhv)2 versus hv for the CN and CNNS samples, and (c) Plot of (αhv)1/2 versus hv for the BiOBr.
TG analysis of the CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, CNNS/BiOBr (20%), and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites with different weight percentages of BiOBr.
Photodegradation of RhB over the CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, CNNS/BiOBr (20%), and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites.
UV–vis spectra for degradation of RhB under visible-light irradiation over the (a) CNNS, (b) CNNS/BiOBr (20%), (c) CNNS/CDs, and (d) CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) samples.

(a) The degradation rate constants of RhB over CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, CNNS/BiOBr (20%), and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites. (b) PL spectra for the CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, CNNS/BiOBr (20%), CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%), and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (40%) samples. (c) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption data for the CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, CNNS/BiOBr (20%), and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) samples.
Mott–Schottky plots for the CN, CNNS, and BiOBr samples.
Proposed Z-scheme mechanism for the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites.
The degradation rate constants of RhB over the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite in presence of various scavengers.

(a) The degradation rate constants of RhB over the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite prepared at different refluxing times. (b) PL spectra for the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite prepared by refluxing for 30, 60 and 240 min.
(a) Reusability of the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite. (b) XRD patterns and (c) of FT-IR spectra of the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite before and after photocatalysis.
The degradation rate constants of RhB, MB, MO and photocatalytic reduction of Cr (VI) over the CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, CNNS/BiOBr (20%), and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) samples under visible-light irradiation.
Schematic illustration for preparation of the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites.
Weight percentages of different elements in the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite.
The textural properties of CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, CNNS/BiOBr (20%), and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets anchored with BiOBr and carbon dots: Exceptional visible-light-driven photocatalytic performances for oxidation and reduction reactions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, P.O. Box 179, Ardabil, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Photocatalysis International Research Center, Tokyo University of Science, 2641 Yamazaki, Noda-shi, Chiba 278-8510, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The aim of this work is the fabrication of very efficient visible-light-driven photocatalysts through anchoring carbon dots and BiOBr over nanosheets of graphitic carbon nitride. Hence, a series of g-C3N4 nanosheets/Carbon dots/BiOBr (denoted as CNNS/CDs/BiOBr) nanocomposites with different amounts of BiOBr were synthesized by refluxing route. The resultant photocatalysts were characterized by XRD, EDX, SEM, TEM, HRTEM, AFM, XPS, FT-IR, UV–vis DRS, TGA, BET, and PL instruments. Among the photocatalysts, the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance for degradations of RhB, MB, and MO under visible light, which is about 129, 29.8, and 20.5 times as superior as the CN powder, respectively. Also, photoreduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) over the ternary nanocomposite with 20% of BiOBr showed high photoactivity, which was 21.7-folds higher than that of the pristine CN. Reactive species trapping experiments revealed that superoxide anion radicals and hydroxyl radicals played significant role in degradation reaction of RhB. The utilized photocatalyst was recycled with negligible loss in the activity, which is important in photocatalytic processes. Finally, by studying the electrochemical behavior, the band alignments of the semiconductors were obtained and the preliminary mechanism was suggested as a direct Z‐scheme system for the enhanced interfacial charge separation and transfer, which leads to the exceptional photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last decades, environmental pollution and energy shortage, due to uncontrolled growth of population and huge development of industries, have attracted intensive attention from research communities worldwide [1–3]. Nowadays, heterogeneous photocatalysis has emerged as a green technology for remediation of environmental pollutants, solar energy conversion, photofixation of nitrogen gas, and synthesis of diverse kinds of organic compounds [4–8]. With the aim of utilizing solar energy in these processes, design and preparation of efficient visible-light-driven (VLD) photocatalysts are highly desirable [9–12].

After pioneering work of Wang in 2009, graphitic carbon nitride (denoted as CN) has attracted tremendous attention from researchers [13]. Cheap price, medium band gap energy, good thermal stability up to almost 520 °C, and excellent chemical stability are some of its appealing properties, which triggered its widespread applications [14,15]. These unique characteristics make this polymeric semiconductor as a promising candidate to fabricate next generation VLD photocatalysts for effectively utilizing solar energy in these processes [16]. Nonetheless, the photocatalytic performance of this organic semiconductor is greatly limited owing to little harvesting of visible light, insufficient textural characteristics, and rapid recombination of the photoinduced charge carriers [17–20]. Consequently, exploration of facile strategies to synthesize CN-based VLD photocatalysts with exceptionally improved photocatalytic performances is urgently necessary. Accordingly, several strategies have been employed to enhance the photocatalytic performance of CN under visible light, including structural engineering, doping with metal/nonmetal species, and anchoring other semiconductors [21–37]. Among two-dimensional materials, nanosheets have attracted extensive attention due to their attractive structural, physicochemical, and textural properties [38–41]. Recently, nanosheets of semiconductors have displayed a brilliant future due to their unique characteristics such as large surface area, extremely thin thickness, and plentiful surface groups for anchoring cocatalysts [42–45].

Compared to the widely investigated composites based on CN, CN/BiOBr and CN/CDs composites have been introduced as promising candidates in recent years for photocatalytic applications under UV–visible irradiation [46–50]. It has been known that modification of CN with CDs can facilitate the transfer of charge carriers and improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges due to its unique electron mediating feature [46,47]. Also coupling of CN with BiOBr can improve the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, leading to enhance of the photocatalytic performance [48–50]. Therefore, in the present work, after exfoliating CN (abbreviated as CNNS) by a facile thermal exfoliation route [51], nanoparticles of CDs and BiOBr were anchored over CNNS to fabricate ternary CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites. Hence, this work is dedicated to design and synthesize these nanocomposites with exceptional VLD photocatalytic performance by a simple method. In addition, with the aim of overcoming the inherent deficiencies of semiconductors such as fast recombination rate of the electron‐hole pairs and low redox ability, a direct Z‐scheme photocatalytic mechanism was suggested [52,53]. Recently, many CN‐based photocatalytic systems with a direct Z‐scheme mechanism have been constructed [54–59]. In the proposed mechanism, the CDs act as an ideal electron transfer mediators to significantly improve the photocatalytic activity [60,61]. Among the photocatalysts, the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite exhibited superior performance in degradations of three organic pollutants containing two cationic (RhB and MB) and one anionic (MO) under visible light. In addition to the organic pollutants, photoreduction of Cr(VI), as a typical heavy metal ion contaminant [62], was done and excellent activity under visible-light illumination was observed for the ternary photocatalyst.

The CN powder was synthesized by a polycondensation reaction of melamine (Loba Chemie, 99.2%) according to the procedure reported elsewhere [51]. For synthesis of the CNNS sample, 2 g CN was powdered in a mortar and placed in a petri dish. Afterward, the powder was heated in air at 520 °C for 2 h [63]. The stable solution of CDs was synthesized using a mixture of citric acid (Merck, 99%) and urea (Merck, 99%) under microwave irradiation for 5 min [64]. The CNNS/CDs nanocomposite was prepared by our reported method [51].

To synthesis of the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite, in which the weight percent of BiOBr was fixed at 20 wt%, Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (Merck, 99%) and NaBr (Merck, 99.8%) were used as the precursors of BiOBr. For this purpose, 0.40 g CNNS/CDs was dissolved in 150 mL of deionized water (DIW) via ultrasonic irradiation for 5 min. Afterward, 0.159 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved into 20 mL of DIW and then it was added into the suspension containing the CNNS/CDs nanocomposite. Thereafter, the mixture was continuously stirred using a magnetic agitator for 2 h. Subsequently, an aqueous solution of 0.034 g NaBr in 20 mL of DIW was dropwise added into the mentioned suspension. Finally, after stirring for another 1 h, the suspension was refluxed for 1 h and the resulting yellow precipitate was collected and washed three times by DIW and ethanol, respectively and then it was dried in an oven at 60 °C overnight. Similarly, the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites with 10, 30, and 40 wt% of BiOBr were synthesized. For comparison, the CNNS/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite was prepared in the same way without adding CDs. The method applied for fabrication of the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites is demonstrated in Scheme 1 .

The XRD data were collected via Cu Kα radiation by a Philips Xpert X-ray diffractometer. The SEM (LEO 1430VP) was conducted with accelerating voltage of 15 kV and HRTEM instrument (EM-002B microscope, TOPCON) was employed by operating at 200 kV. The thickness was assessed by an AFM microscope (Dualscope/Rasterscope C26, DME, Denmark). The EDX spectra and elemental mapping were recorded using the same SEM. The elemental state of the ternary nanocomposite was analyzed by XPS (JPS-9010MC, JEOL) using non-monochromated Mg Kα radiation (1253.6 eV). The TGA plots were conducted on Linseis STA PT 1000 apparatus with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in the air. The UV–vis DR spectra of the samples were recorded using a Scinco 4100 apparatus. The infrared absorption spectra were provided by a Perkin Elmer Spectrum RX I spectrophotometer. The PL spectra of the samples were investigated by a Perkin Elmer (LS 55) fluorescence spectrometer. The BET textural characteristics were analyzed with a BELSORP-mini II adsorption apparatus at −196 °C. The UV–vis spectra were provided using a Cecile 9000 spectrophotometer. The electrochemical tests were recorded via an EIS spectrometer (µAutolabIII/FRA2 with Nova software) in a standard three-electrode glassy cell. A Bandelin ultrasonic instrument (9HD 3100) was used (probe diameter = 12 mm, Ti horn, 75 W, working frequency = 20 kHz) for ultrasonic irradiations.

The photocatalytic investigations were conducted in a cylindrical glassy reactor with a water circulation system to adjust temperature of the reaction suspension at 25 °C. A 50 W LED lamp was positioned on the top of the photoreactor to provide visible light. In the typical photocatalysis reaction, 250 mL aqueous solution containing the organic pollutant (RhB, MB, and MO with the concentration of 1 × 10–5 M) and 0.1 g of the photocatalyst were put in the reactor with an air bubbling system and a mechanical stirrer to keep the suspension homogeneous. The suspension was kept in dark condition to reach equilibrium and then the lamp was switched on to start the photocatalysis reaction. The remained concentrations of the pollutants were analyzed after removing the photocatalyst using a centrifuge by measuring the absorbances of RhB, MB, and MO at 553, 664, and 477 nm, respectively.

For evaluation photocatalytic activity of the samples for reduction of Cr(VI), similar conditions were conducted. The Cr(VI) solution (100 mg/L) was prepared by dissolving K2Cr2O7 in DIW. The photocatalyst (0.1 g) was dispersed in 250 mL of Cr(VI) solution and after stirring for 60 min in dark condition, the suspension was exposed to the visible-light irradiation. The diphenylcarbazide photometric method was used to analyze the concentration of Cr(VI) at 540 nm [65].

The electrochemical measurements were recorded via an EIS spectrometer in a standard three-electrode glassy cell by applying 10 mV alternative signal in the frequency range 1 MHz to 100 mHz. The photocatalysts, coated with fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) glass, were as the working electrodes, a Pt sheet as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode and the utilized electrolyte was 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. For fabrication of working electrodes, 1 mg of the photocatalyst powder was suspended in 0.1 mL of deionized water. To produce a slurry, the mixture was ultrasonically scattered for 20 min. Then, the slurry was dripped on a 1.5 × 1.0 cm FTO glass electrode. Then, the electrode was dried at room temperature. Finally, 10 μL of 0.5 wt% Nafion® 117 solutions (Sigma-Aldrich, 5 wt%), as a binder on the FTO, was deposited onto the modified electrode.

Phase purity of the synthesized photocatalysts was verified by XRD patterns, as observed in Fig. 1 . The CN and CNNS samples demonstrate two diffraction peaks at 13.5° and 27.5°, which were indexed to (1 0 0) and (0 0 2) planes, corresponding to in-planar and interlayer stacking, respectively (JCPDS No. 87-1526) [51]. Due to the low amount of CDs introduced over the CN, similar XRD pattern was obtained for the CNNS/CDs nanocomposite [66]. In the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites, besides the diffraction peaks of CNNS, the peaks for BiOBr were clearly observed, which were in good accordance with JCPDS file number 73-2061 [67]. Additionally, no other peaks were observed, belonging to any impurities.

Purity and elemental composition of the CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) photocatalysts were explored by EDX spectroscopy and the spectra are illustrated in Fig. 2 a. In addition, element mappings for the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite are illustrated in Fig. 2(b–g). In the CN and CNNS photocatalysts, the peaks of C and N elements can be observed, indicating that these photocatalysts have composed of these elements. Likewise, the EDX spectrum of CNNS/CDs nanocomposite shows peaks corresponding to C, N, and O elements, due to the presence of functional groups such as OH, CO, and COOH in CDs [51]. The CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite not only displays the main peaks for C, N, and O elements, but also gives the peaks of Bi and Br elements, which indicates that BiOBr has been attached to the surface of CNNS/CDs nanocomposite to construct the ternary CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite. As shown in the element mappings, all of the five elements are observed in the selected area of the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite, confirming formation of the ternary nanocomposite. Weight percentages of elements in the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite were calculated and are summarized in Table 1 , which are in good accordance with the theoretical values.

The morphology and nanostructure of the CNNS and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite were investigated by SEM, TEM, and HRTEM instruments. As observed in Fig. 3 a, the CNNS sample exhibits excellent exfoliation after thermal treatment of the bulk CN, which is more desirable. Fig. 3b demonstrates that CDs and BiOBr nanoparticles accumulated on the surface of CNNS, proving the formation of the heterostructure. Additionally, Fig. 3c shows TEM image of the ternary nanocomposite. It is observed that CDs and BiOBr particles have deposited on the surface of CNNS. In addition, HRTEM image displays the existence of heterojunction between the anchored nanoparticles and CNNS. It is clear that CNNS, CDs, and BiOBr display different orientations and lattice spacing. The obtained HRTEM image clearly revealed that the CNNS, CDs, and BiOBr have interplanar spacings of 0.325 nm [68], 0.320 nm [66], and 0.280 nm [69], respectively, which agree well with the crystallographic (0 0 2) plane of CNNS, (0 0 2) plane of CDs, and (0 1 2) plane of BiOBr, which is favorable for the transfer of the charge carrier.

To observe the thickness of the CNNS sample and confirm successful exfoliation of the bulk CN, AFM measurement was carried out. Fig. 4 a presents AFM image of the resulting CNNS sheets, revealing that they are thin layer structures of CNNS, which well separated from each other. Also, due to the average thickness of the CNNS, which is about 1.3–1.6 nm and by considering the theoretical thickness of a single layer (0.34 nm) [70], it can be inferred that four or five layers of ultrathin CN nanosheets were successfully fabricated (Fig. 4b). As a result, ultrathin nature of the CNNS sample is confirmed by AFM analysis.

Chemical characters of the elements presented in the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) photocatalyst were investigated by means of XPS instrument, as the spectra are shown in Fig. 5 . A full spectrum of the ternary photocatalyst indicated the peaks corresponding to C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, Bi 4f, and Br 3d and no other characteristic peaks for other elements were detected, which approves purity of the ternary photocatalyst (Fig. 5a). The peaks of C 1s, centered in the range of 280–294 eV, were deconvoluted into three different peaks centered at 285.0, 289.7, and 286.2 eV, corresponding to sp2 (CC), CNH2 group of the broad asymmetric C 1s feature of CNNS, and COH of CDs particles, respectively (Fig. 5b) [71–73]. The spectrum of O 1 s shows three distinct prominent peal peaks at 530.8, 532.2, and 534.0 eV, which correspond to the oxygen of BiO, COC, and CO groups, respectively (Fig. 5c) [74,75]. The N 1s spectrum in Fig. 5d appeared two peaks at 398.2 and 400.4 eV, which attributed to CNC and the N–H groups, respectively, belonging to CNNS [76]. For the Bi 4f spectrum, two individual symmetric peaks were appeared at 159.5 and 164.8 eV, assigning to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively, which is a characteristic of Bi3+ in BiOBr semiconductor (Fig. 5e) [74]. The two peaks of Br element at 68.6 and 69.3 eV are ascribed to Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2, respectively (Fig. 5f) [77]. Hence, the XPS analysis, in accordance with the XRD, EDX, and HRTEM analyses, confirms deposition of CDs and BiOBr particles on the surface of CNNS.

The FT-IR spectra of CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, CNNS/BiOBr (20%), and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) samples were provided, as the spectra are represented in Fig. 6 . As observed, all of the photocatalysts display broad absorption peaks at 2900–3300 cm-1 region, which are assigned to the stretching vibrational modes of NH and OH groups. The peaks in the 1200–1600 cm−1 region were related to the stretching vibration of the C-N heterocycles, while the peak at 802 cm−1 was the typical breathing vibration of heptazine units [78]. For the CNNS/CDs and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposites, the peaks at the ranges of 1600–1770 cm−1 and 1350–1460 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CH2 groups presented in CDs, respectively [64,79]. Besides characteristic peaks of CNNS and CDs, the vibration peak of Bi-O bond at 519 cm−1 was observed in the spectra of CNNS/BiOBr (20%) and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposites, which was ascribed to BiOBr [49].

To explore optical characteristics of the photocatalysts, their UV–vis DR spectra were measured and they are illustrated in Fig. 7 a. In comparison with the bulk CN, the absorption threshold of CNNS photocatalyst demonstrated a slight blue-shift with the edge of 456 nm, which proves exfoliation of the fabricated CNNS [80]. After anchoring CDs on the surface of CNNS, the absorbance in visible region was slightly improved. Fig. 7a displays better visible-light capturing ability of the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites when compared with the CNNS and CNNS/BiOBr (20%) photocatalysts, indicating their potential to have considerable photocatalytic activity under visible light.

The band gaps of the semiconductors were calculated using Tauc plot, which is related to the relationship between optical absorption coefficient of a semiconductor and the band gap energy as follows:
(1)
α h υ
=
β
h υ -
E g
n / 2
in which α, h, υ, β, and Eg are absorption coefficient, Planck constant, the light frequency, proportionality constant, and band gap energy, respectively. In this equation, the value of n is related to the characteristics of the transition in the semiconductor, which are 4 and 1 for indirect and direct transitions, respectively [81]. In this method, the Eg values for CN and CNNS were estimated by extrapolation of the linear parts of the curves obtained by plotting (αhυ)2 versus hv, because of direct characteristics of CN and CNNS samples, as observed in Fig. 7b. As seen in Fig. 7c, the Eg value for BiOBr was calculated through plotting (αhυ)1/2 versus hv, due to its indirect optical transitions. Consequently, the Eg of the CN, CNNS, and BiOBr are 2.70, 2.80, and 2.88 eV, respectively.

Fig. 8 illustrates TGA plots for the fabricated photocatalysts to obtain information about their thermal stability and CN contents. As can be seen, the photocatalysts show the first slight weight loss by heating up to nearly 140 °C, which originate from the removal of the adsorbed H2O molecules. The TG profile for the CN photocatalyst shows rapid weight loss region started from about 520 °C and extended up to 670 °C [51]. Although the TG profiles for various samples were similar to that of the CN, the thermo stability of the photocatalysts diminished by converting CN to CNNS and after integration with CDs and BiOBr, because of providing more active sites for decomposition reaction with oxygen molecules [82,83]. The CN weight percentages obtained from the plots for the CNNS/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite and the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites with 10, 20, 30, and 40% of BiOBr were 77.3, 87.2, 78.6, 66.9, and 58.4%, respectively.

Photocatalytic performances of the CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, CNNS/BiOBr (20%), and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites with 10, 20, 30, and 40% of BiOBr under visible light towards degradation reaction of RhB are illustrated in Fig. 9 . As displayed in the blank experiment, the photolysis of RhB without any photocatalyst is poor, which confirms that the decrease of RhB in the solutions is mainly attributed to the photocatalysis rather than photolysis. It is evident that the ternary nanocomposites show improved photocatalytic performance relative to the pure CN. Among the ternary photocatalysts, the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite provided exceptional photocatalytic performance and completely degraded RhB within a short time of 30 min. To investigate the adsorptive ability of the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite for RhB, the adsorption test over this sample was done in dark condition. As seen, it is evident that in absence of the light, the adsorption process reached to equilibrium after stirring the suspension for 60 min and then the concentration did not show any changes. Therefore, in the photocatalytic tests, decrease of the RhB concentration after dark condition for 60 min is related to the degradation process rather than adsorption.

Fig. 10 (a–d) show the spectral changes for solutions of RhB mediated by CNNS, CNNS/BiOBr (20%), CNNS/CDs, and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) photocatalysts under visible-light. As it is seen, the absorption peak of RhB, located at 553 nm, decreased with increasing the irradiation time. Furthermore, in the presence of CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite, no absorbance peak was observed after the light irradiation for 30 min, which indicated complete decomposition of RhB. While at the same time, 20.2, 26.6, and 36.8% of RhB were degraded over the CNNS, CNNS/BiOBr (20%), and CNNS/CDs photocatalysts, respectively. Additionally, there is not any shift in the spectra during the degradation reactions over the selected photocatalysts, representing aromatic-ring degradation mechanism for the pollutant [51].

To calculate the observed degradation rate constant (kobs ) of the pollutant, the degradation data were fitted in a first-order kinetic model as expressed by Eq. (2).
(2)
ln
C /
C
0
= -
k
obs
t
where C0 and C are concentrations of RhB in solutions after the light irradiation for 0 and t minutes, respectively. Fig. 11 a shows that the BiOBr content greatly influenced on the degradation reaction and the ternary nanocomposite with 20 wt% BiOBr exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance, which was about 129, 23.2, and 10.2-folds higher than that of the CN, CNNS, and CNNS/CDs samples, respectively. However, for the ternary nanocomposite with BiOBr contents higher than 20 wt%, the photocatalytic activity was significantly decreased.

As known, PL spectroscopy is a well-established method to explore the extent of charge carrier separation in photocatalysts. Thus, PL spectra for the CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, CNNS/BiOBr (20%), and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr photocatalysts with 10, 20, and 30% of BiOBr were provided, as represented in Fig. 11b. As the intensity of the PL signal is proportional the recombination rate of the charge carriers, it can be found that the PL signal for the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites was greatly quenched relative to the CN sample, implying that the photogenerated e−/h+ pairs have better separation at the interface between the counterparts. The suitable BiOBr content caused well separation of the charge carriers is 20%, which favored exceptional photocatalytic activity.

In this step, N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms for the CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, CNNS/BiOBr (20%), and CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) photocatalysts were measured to characterize their textural properties. As seen in Fig. 11c, the isotherms exhibited type IV behaviors, indicating the presence of mesoporous structures. The calculated specific surface area, pore diameter, and total pore volume of the samples are collected in Table 2 . It is clear that the CNNS has high specific surface area (88.1 m2 g−1) compared with the bulk CN (14.6 m2 g−1), reflecting high degree of exfoliation during the preparation procedure. However, after anchoring CDs and BiOBr particles, the surface area of CNNS photocatalyst was decreased, which is ascribed to the blocking some of the active sites [63]. Although the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) photocatalyst does not have the greatest surface area, it showed the premier photocatalytic performance in the degradation reaction. These results demonstrated that in the improved photocatalytic performance of the ternary photocatalyst relative to the counterparts, production of a large number of charge carriers and effective suppression of these charge from recombination have major role relative to the surface area.

The Mott–Schottky method was employed to determine the physicochemical properties of the CN, CNNS, and BiOBr, including the semiconductor type, the flat-band potential (Efb ), Fermi level (Ef ), and band alignments of semiconductors. If the slope of the linear part of Mott–Schottky curve is negative, the semiconductor is p-type. Contrarily, the slope of n-type semiconductor is positive [84]. From Fig. 12 , positive slopes were evidently observed in the Mott–Schottky curve of the CN, CNNS, and BiOBr samples, implying they were n-type semiconductors. Furthermore, the Efb values were assessed from the x-intercept by drawing the tangents of the Mott–Schottky curves [85]. Consideration of the Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, the Efb values were −1.23, −1.35, and 0.13 eV vs. Ag/AgCl for the CN, CNNS, and BiOBr samples, respectively. The measured potentials vs. Ag/AgCl were converted to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) scale using ENHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.2 [86]. After converting to NHE scale, the Efb values became −1.03, −1.15, and 0.33 eV vs. NHE for the CN, CNNS, and BiOBr, respectively. In general, the measured Efb in Mott–Schottky curve is regarded as the Ef of semiconductor. As we know, the bottom of the conduction band (ECB ) of n-type semiconductor is more negative than Ef and the top of the valence band (EVB ) of p-type semiconductor is more positive than Ef . It is known that the bottom of the ECB in many n-type semiconductors is more negative by ∼−0.1 V than the Efb
[87]. Hence, ECB of the CN, CNNS, and BiOBr can be calculate to -1.13, -1.25, and 0.23 eV, respectively. Afterward, using the Eg values obtained from Tauc plots and the ECB values obtained from Mott–Schottky plots, the EVB of the semiconductors calculated using the following empirical formula:
(4)
E
VB
=
E
CB
+
E g
Consequently, the EVB of the CN, CNNS, and BiOBr samples were calculated as 1.57, 1.55, and 3.11 eV, respectively.

Based on the results, a plausible direct Z-scheme mechanism was proposed for the substantially enhanced photocatalytic performances happened on the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites as illustrated in Fig. 13 . Due to the band potentials obtained from Mott–Schottky method, it is observed that the two constituent semiconductors of CNNS and BiOBr have well-staggered band alignment. Hence, in the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites, both the band potentials and formed heterojunctions between the constituents are favorable for separation of the charges. When CNNS/CDs/BiOBr nanocomposites are excited under visible light, electrons in the VB of CNNS and BiOBr are excited to their CBs and equal numbers of holes are generated in their VBs. The photogenerated electrons in the CB of BiOBr are able to migrate quickly to the surface of CDs and further transfer to the VB of CNNS, due to good electron donor and electron acceptor characteristics of CDs [60,61]. As a result, electrons and holes are accumulated in the CB of CNNS and VB of BiOBr, respectively, which leads to reduction of recombination rate for the charge carries within CNNS and BiOBr, participating in the degradation reactions. So, over this ternary photocatalyst, superoxide anion radicals (
O2 −) are produced through reduction reaction of O2 molecules over the CB of CNNS, because of more negative reduction potential of CNNS (ECB = −1.25 eV) compared with O2/
O2 − (E0(O2/
O2 −) = −0.33 eV/NHE) [88]. Hence, the photoinduced holes on the VB of BiOBr can produce
OH radicals through oxidation of –OH and H2O, because the VB potential of BiOBr (+3.11 eV) is more positive than the potentials of OH−/OH
(+2.69 eV) and H2O/OH
(+2.72 eV) systems [88,89]. Therefore, the generated
O2 − and OH
subsequently participate in the photocatalytic degradation of dyes.

It has been reported that
O2 −, h+, and
OH species could have the significant role in photocatalytic oxidation reactions. Hence, to investigate the photocatalytic degradation mechanism over the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) photocatalyst, several scavengers were introduced to the reaction system with concentration of 1.0 mM to explore the role of these reactive species in the photocatalytic degradation reaction and the results are displayed in Fig. 14 . As observed, addition of benzoquinone (BQ) considerably suppressed the photocatalytic reaction, in which the degradation rate constant decreased from 1393 × 10-4 min−1 to 30.2 × 10−4 min−1. It can be concluded that during the degradation process,
O2
– species are the major reactive species. Besides, in the presence of 2-propanol (2-PrOH, a scavenger of •OH) and ammonium oxalate (AO, a scavenger of h+), the degradation rate constant decreased from 1393 × 10−4 min−1 to 363 × 10−4 min−1 and 655 × 10−4 min−1, respectively. Therefore, it can be inferred that the inhibition degree follows the order BQ > 2-PrOH > AO, revealing that
O2 − and
OH species play the most important role in the photocatalytic degradation process.

Among various techniques for preparing photocatalysts, refluxing method is a convenient, cheap, and very effective process for large-scale production. In this work, we investigated the effect of refluxing times on the photoactivity of the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite. Fig. 15 a displays that the degradation efficiency of RhB enhances with increasing refluxing time up to 60 min. When the refluxing time reached to 120 min, the degradation rate constant decreased. To justify these changes, PL spectra of the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite was provided to study the recombination rate of charge carriers over this photocatalyst prepared by refluxing for 30, 60 and 240 min (Fig. 15b). As seen, the lowest PL intensity was indicated for the photocatalyst refluxed for 60 min, which leads to the best photocatalytic performance.

Considering the industrial utilization of a photocatalyst, its stability is highly important. To test the stability of CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite, it was collected after photodegradation experiments for five cycles. As shown in Fig. 16 a, the photocatalyst presents good stability in these experiments with small deactivation. Moreover, to prove the stability, the XRD patterns of the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite before and after 5th run have been provided (Fig. 16b). It can be clearly observed that the phase and structure of the nanocomposite have not changed, which suggests that the sample possesses a good photostability during the photocatalytic reaction. Also, the functional groups of the CNNS/CDs/BiOCl (20%) nanocomposite before and after photocatalysis were characterized using FT-IR analysis and the results are illustrated in Fig. 16c, clearly confirming the structural stability during the degradation reaction. Noteworthy, high structural stability and substantially enhanced performance of the ternary nanocomposite make it a promising VLD photocatalyst.

The capability of a photocatalyst to degrade widespread pollutants presented in real wastewaters is a practical problem of heterogeneous photocatalytic processes. As a consequence, photocatalytic performance of the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite in the photocatalytic removal of RhB and MB (as cationic pollutants) and MO (as anionic pollutant) along with photoreduction reaction of Cr(VI) (as an inorganic pollutant) were compared with its constituents, as the results are observed in Fig. 17 (a–d). Interestingly, the ternary photocatalyst has exceptional activity in photocatalytic removal of the selected organic and inorganic pollutants through oxidation and reduction reactions. The rate constants over the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite in degradations of RhB, MB, and MO are 129, 29.8, and 20.5 times as large as that of the CN photocatalyst, respectively. Also, highly improved activity was observed for the photoreduction reaction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) over the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite, which is 21.7, 9.6, 4.6, and 6.8 times as high as the CN, CNNS, CNNS/CDs, and CNNS/BiOBr (20%) photocatalysts, respectively. As a result, we concluded that the CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite has exceptional photocatalytic performance under visible light to be used in real environmental remediation process with substantial stability.

In a brief, novel highly efficient Z-scheme ternary CNNS/CDs/BiOBr photocatalysts were prepared via a facile refluxing method at low temperature and short preparation time. These photocatalysts were applied in photodegradation reactions of RhB, MB, and MO dyes and also in photoreduction reaction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) under visible light. It was revealed that content of BiOBr and refluxing time had considerable effect on the photocatalytic performance. The CNNS/CDs/BiOBr (20%) nanocomposite exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance for degradations of RhB, MB, and MO under visible light, which was about 129, 29.8, and 20.5 times as superior as the pristine CN, respectively, and also, photoreduction of Cr(VI) over the ternary nanocomposite had photoactivity equal to 21.7-folds higher than that of the CN. The substantially improved photocatalytic performance of the ternary nanocomposite was mainly attributed to separation of the charges, as confirmed by PL analysis. In addition, the ternary nanocomposite displayed excellent reusability and stability, which is valuable for the potential environmental applications. A plausible Z-scheme mechanism for the improved photoactivity was suggested by promoting charge transfer between CNNS and BiOBr through CDs particles. This work provides a facile method for fabrication of very efficient CN-based heterojunction photocatalysts with substantial activity in photocatalytic oxidation and photocatalytic reduction reactions applied for removal of organic and inorganic pollutants, respectively.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors genuinely acknowledge University of Mohaghegh Ardabili for financial support of this work.
